description: This figure presents field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) secondary-electron images and corresponding energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental maps characterizing the morphology and phase constitution of non-metallic inclusions in the 1# Fe-15Mn-1Al-C low-density steel. Panels (a) through (e) show single-particle inclusions: an elongated Al₂O₃ particle, an approximately elliptical AlN inclusion, an extracted elongated AlN crystal, a monolithic MnS inclusion, and an angular TiN particle. Panels (f) through (i) display composite inclusions, including a core-shell Al₂O₃-MnS structure, an AlN-MnS agglomerate with accompanying EDS maps confirming localized Al, N, Mn, and S signals, a TiN-MnS composite exhibiting a distinct interfacial boundary, and a complex Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS inclusion with EDS mappings showing spatial distributions of Mn, S, Al, N, and O. The micrographs collectively demonstrate that inclusions in this steel exist as both discrete particles and heterogeneous multi-phase aggregates, providing direct evidence for sequential precipitation and heterogeneous nucleation during solidification.

This figure presents FE-SEM micrographs and corresponding EDS elemental mappings characterizing the morphology and types of inclusions in sample 2# (Fe-15Mn-3Al-C low-density steel). Subfigures (a)-(c) show single-particle inclusions: (a) an elongated AlN inclusion approximately 23 μm in size, (b) an octahedral MnS particle exhibiting a distinct three-dimensional morphology approximately 10 μm, and (c) an irregular boot-shaped TiN inclusion. Subfigures (d)-(g) display composite inclusions with specific interfacial configurations: (d) an Al2O3-MnS composite where dark Al2O3 is partially enveloped by gray MnS; (e) an AlN-MnS composite demonstrating tight binding between the two phases, accompanied by EDS maps confirming Al/N-rich and Mn/S-rich regions; (f) a three-layer Al2O3-AlN-MnS composite with Al2O3 at the core, AlN in the middle, and MnS at the outermost layer; and (g) a TiN-MnS composite with corresponding S, Ti, Mn, and N EDS mappings delineating the TiN phase within the MnS matrix. These observations collectively demonstrate that earlier precipitated phases serve as heterogeneous nucleation sites for subsequent precipitation, forming core-shell or tightly bound complex inclusions during solidification.

This figure presents the three-dimensional morphology and elemental verification of non-metallic inclusions extracted from the 3# Fe-15Mn-5Al-C low-density steel sample (highest Al content), observed via field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping. Subfigures (a) and (b) display AlN inclusions exhibiting polyhedral single and multiple particles, with EDS maps confirming coincident Al and N distributions. Subfigures (c) and (d) show octahedral MnS inclusions approximately 10 μm in size, verified by overlapping Mn and S elemental maps. Subfigures (e) and (f) reveal elongated TiN inclusions with lengths exceeding 30 μm, supported by corresponding Ti and N mappings. Subfigures (g) through (i) illustrate composite inclusions: (g) shows an Al2O3-MnS composite with Al2O3 at the bottom and MnS above, confirmed by Al, O, S, and Mn maps; (h) presents a TiN-MnS composite in a cigarette-bag-like morphology; and (i) shows an AlN-MnS composite with close interfacial binding, validated by Al, N, S, and Mn distributions. The EDS Fe map in (g) shows a signal void corresponding to the inclusion, consistent with extraction from the steel matrix. Collectively, these images demonstrate that the 3# sample contains single-phase AlN, MnS, and TiN inclusions as well as complex composite inclusions formed via heterogeneous nucleation, supporting the solidification sequence and interfacial mismatch analysis.

This stacked bar chart (Figure 4) presents the number percentage distribution of non-metallic inclusions across three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples (1#, 2#, 3#) with progressively increasing Al content. Each vertical bar is segmented by inclusion type—single-phase Al₂O₃, AlN, MnS, TiN and composite Al₂O₃-MnS, AlN-MnS, TiN-MnS, and Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS—as identified by FE-SEM/EDS. Visually, the single Al₂O₃ fraction drops markedly from 1# to 3#, whereas the single AlN fraction rises substantially. The MnS proportion first increases from 1# to 2# then declines in 3#, while TiN remains nearly unchanged. Among composite inclusions, Al₂O₃-MnS increases with Al content, whereas AlN-MnS and TiN-MnS decrease. Notably, Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS inclusions appear in 1# and 2# but are absent in 3#. These trends demonstrate that raising Al content shifts the dominant inclusion phase from Al₂O₃ to AlN and alters composite inclusion formation, reflecting modified solidification routes and delayed precipitation of MnS and TiN.

Figure 5 presents a grouped bar chart showing the size distribution of non-metallic inclusions in three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples (1#, 2#, and 3#, corresponding to Al contents of approximately 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt%, respectively), as quantified by an automatic inclusion analysis system. The horizontal axis divides inclusions into three size intervals: 1–5 μm, 5–10 μm, and >10 μm, while the vertical axis reports the number percentage (%). For the 1–5 μm range, the number percentage decreases from 58.4% in 1# to 53.6% in 2# and 48.2% in 3#. Conversely, the 5–10 μm fraction increases monotonically with Al content, from 32.3% (1#) to 34.6% (2#) and 46.9% (3#). The >10 μm category shows a non-monotonic trend: 9.4% in 1#, rising to 11.8% in 2#, then dropping to 4.9% in 3#. These data demonstrate that increasing Al content shifts the inclusion population toward intermediate sizes (5–10 μm) while reducing the finest fraction (1–5 μm), supporting the conclusion that the solidification route change and delayed precipitation kinetics alter the growth and size distribution of inclusions in these steels.

This figure presents FactSage 8.2-calculated vertical section phase diagrams of the Fe-C-Si-Mn-P-S-N-O-Al-Ti system for three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steels: (a) 1# with approximately 0.87 wt% Al, (b) 2# with approximately 2.77 wt% Al, and (c) 3# with approximately 4.70 wt% Al. The diagrams plot temperature (T, K) versus carbon content (wt%), with a green vertical line marking the fixed alloy carbon content near 0.41 wt%. Red and gray dots indicate the calculated liquidus and solidus temperatures, respectively. The 1# sample solidifies through the FCC phase field via the route L → L+FCC → FCC. In contrast, the 2# and 3# samples solidify via routes that pass through the BCC phase field: L → L+BCC → L+BCC+FCC → L+FCC → FCC for 2#, and L → L+BCC → L+BCC+FCC → BCC+FCC → FCC for 3#. As the Al content increases from 1# to 3#, the liquidus temperature rises while the solidus temperature drops, thereby narrowing the austenite (FCC) region and expanding the ferrite (BCC) region. This change in solidification route delays the precipitation of AlN, MnS, and TiN inclusions during solidification.

The figure presents the calculated relationship between the logarithm of the equilibrium constant logK_AlN (solid lines) and the logarithm of the actual concentration product logQ_AlN (dashed lines) as a function of solidification fraction z_s for three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steels. Black, blue, and red curves correspond to samples 1# (Fe-15Mn-1Al-C), 2# (Fe-15Mn-3Al-C), and 3# (Fe-15Mn-5Al-C), respectively. The intersections of the solid and dashed curves mark the solidification fractions at which AlN inclusions precipitate, yielding values of 0.46 for 1#, 0.22 for 2#, and 0.51 for 3#. The data reveal a non-monotonic trend: increasing Al content from 1% to 3% advances AlN precipitation (fraction decreases from 0.46 to 0.22), whereas further increasing Al to 5% delays precipitation (fraction rises to 0.51). This behavior reflects the combined effects of Al and N solute segregation and the shift in solidification route with varying Al content, supporting the conclusion that Al addition non-monotonically governs the precipitation sequence of AlN inclusions during solidification.

Figure 8 presents the calculated segregation ratios (c_L/c_0) of Al and N as a function of solidification fraction (z_s) for three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples: 1# (x≈0.87 wt% Al), 2# (x≈2.77 wt% Al), and 3# (x≈4.70 wt% Al). The main panel displays six curves: solid lines denote Al segregation and dashed lines denote N segregation for each sample. An inset magnifies the region 0.90 ≤ z_s ≤ 1.00 to highlight the sharp upturn in segregation ratios near the end of solidification. The plot visually demonstrates that the degree of N segregation is markedly higher than that of Al across all samples. As z_s increases from 0 to 1.0, the solute enrichment in the residual liquid gradually rises and accelerates in the final stage. The citation notes that the combined segregation behavior of Al and N promotes AlN inclusion precipitation, and in the 3# sample, decreased N solubility delays the solidification fraction at which AlN forms. These thermodynamic calculation results align with the FactSage 8.2 solidification path analysis reported in the study.

This line chart presents the relationship among the logarithm of the equilibrium constant (log K_TiN), the logarithm of the concentration product (log Q_TiN), and the solidification fraction (z_s) for TiN inclusion precipitation in Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steels. Three alloys are compared: 1# (Fe-15Mn-1Al-C), 2# (Fe-15Mn-3Al-C), and 3# (Fe-15Mn-5Al-C). Solid curves denote log K_TiN, while dashed curves denote log Q_TiN for each sample. The intersections indicate the precipitation solidification fractions of TiN inclusions, which are 0.89 for 1#, 0.93 for 2#, and 0.94 for 3#. As Al content increases, the log Q_TiN curves shift upward and intersect the corresponding log K_TiN curves at progressively higher z_s values, demonstrating that higher Al content delays TiN precipitation during solidification. This trend is attributed to the altered solidification route and solute segregation behavior with increasing aluminum, as supported by FactSage 8.2 thermodynamic calculations.

Figure 10 presents the thermodynamic calculation of MnS precipitation during solidification in Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steels, showing the relationship between logK_MnS, logQ_MnS, and solidification fraction z_s. Solid lines denote the logarithm of equilibrium solubility product logK for samples 1# (Fe-15Mn-1Al-C), 2# (Fe-15Mn-3Al-C), and 3# (Fe-15Mn-5Al-C), all decreasing with z_s. Dashed lines represent the logarithm of actual concentration product logQ, increasing with z_s. The intersections determine the precipitation solidification fractions of MnS inclusions: 0.77 for 1#, 0.81 for 2#, and 0.85 for 3#. As Al content increases from 1% to 5%, the intersection shifts rightward, indicating delayed MnS precipitation. This trend correlates with the elevated liquidus and reduced solidus temperatures, narrowed austenite region, and altered solidification path calculated by FactSage 8.2, supporting the conclusion that higher Al content postpones MnS inclusion formation.

This figure displays a chart of calculated two-dimensional mismatch degrees for composite inclusion interfaces in Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steels. The plot contains four data points corresponding to the interfaces Al2O3/MnS, AlN/MnS, TiN/MnS, and Al2O3/AlN, with the vertical axis showing mismatch values in percent. The chart is horizontally divided into three zones: Most effective zone below 6%, Moderate effective zone between 6% and 12%, and Invalid zone above 12%. Visually, all four points lie within the Moderate effective zone, with Al2O3/MnS and AlN/MnS positioned at lower mismatch values while TiN/MnS and Al2O3/AlN are located near the upper boundary. The caption and citation sentences report the specific values as 8.41%, 8.94%, 11.65%, and 11.66%, respectively. These results indicate that Al2O3, AlN, and TiN can serve as heterogeneous nucleation substrates for MnS, and Al2O3 can also provide nucleation sites for AlN, supporting the formation mechanism of composite inclusions observed in the steel samples.

This schematic diagram (Figure 12) depicts the evolution of non-metallic inclusions during smelting and solidification in Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steels containing 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt% Al. On the left, black faceted and needle-like particles represent single Al₂O₃ inclusions formed during smelting. Along the horizontal "Liquidus" marker, distinct icons denote solidification precipitates: gray hexagonal or polyhedral AlN, gray irregular MnS, and brown angular TiN. Blue arrows link these primary particles to the dashed enclosure on the right, illustrating how early precipitates provide heterogeneous nucleation sites for later ones, yielding composite inclusions. At 1 wt% Al, abundant Al₂O₃ fosters multi-phase aggregates including Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS. At 3 wt% Al, changed solidification conditions produce altered single-particle geometries and composites such as AlN-MnS. The 5 wt% Al row displays diverse isolated single-particle morphologies but no composite-inclusion panel, matching the caption report that Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS composites are absent because of scarce single-particle Al₂O₃. Overall, the figure conveys that increasing Al content shifts the liquidus and solidification path, delays MnS and TiN precipitation, and controls inclusion agglomeration behavior.
